turns.content: I'm so tired of my CI failing on missing arguments. It's like Python where you get a TypeError if I don't pass the right keyword, but here in Rust it just refuses to compile and I have no idea why. Can you explain how this is similar to that scripting pain?

That frustration comes from losing the runtime hint of Python's optional parameters. In Rust, you want to enforce those constraints at compile time so the build never fails because a user forgot a field. A full proc macro can generate a builder pattern that makes every required field mandatory until explicitly set.
I think I understand the idea but not how it actually works under the hood. Is this just like implementing a trait manually or something more magical?

It is neither manual nor purely magical; it's a full proc macro that runs during compilation to expand your derive attribute into actual code. You define the builder logic once in the macro, then every struct using #[derive(MyBuilder)] gets transformed into a fluent API with compile-time checks.
Okay so I'm writing this macro? Does it need to implement any specific traits or interact with serde_json::Value?

You will likely use syn crate to parse the input structs and quote crate to emit the builder code. You do not need serde_json::Value directly unless you want to generate serialization helpers, but the core logic stays within syn's AST representation.
This sounds complicated for my first Rust project. Can I just use existing crates instead of writing a custom derive?

There are crates like builder-pattern or similar that provide basic builders, but they lack the compile-time enforcement you need. A full proc macro gives you exact control over which fields must be present and how errors surface.
I still don't get why this matters for my CI pipeline. Isn't just adding a validation check enough?

A runtime check in your code will fail the build only if you run that specific function; but with our proc macro, any missing required field becomes an immediate compilation error, preventing broken configs from ever reaching CI.
Wait a minute. If I just add a validation method in my struct, why can't I do that instead of this whole macro thing?

Because you need the builder pattern itself to enforce the order and presence of fields automatically during construction. A simple validation method does not prevent users from skipping steps; our proc macro generates a builder where omitting required fields is literally impossible at compile time.
I see now, but what happens if I make an optional field? How do the macros know which ones are required?

The macro scans your struct definition and marks specific fields as required while leaving others optional. When a user tries to build without a required field, the generated code produces a compile-time error instead of silently accepting an incomplete config.
